answers.value:
I am submitting this complaint regarding issues in COSC2627 Programming Fundamentals, Lab Group L02, taught by Associate Lecturer John Carter during Semester 1, 2026. The problems have been ongoing since approximately Week 3 (around 2026-03-15) and have continued through to Week 10.

Summary of main concerns:
1. Repeated cancellations and late starts of scheduled classes without adequate notice
2. Inconsistent and unclear assessment instructions leading to confusion and lost marks
3. Dismissive and, at times, unprofessional communication style from the teacher when students seek clarification
4. Unequal treatment of students in terms of extensions and marking leniency

1. Repeated cancellations and late starts
On at least four occasions (Weeks 3, 4, 6, and 8), our 10:30–12:30 lab session was either cancelled at the last minute or started more than 25 minutes late. In two of those weeks, we received an email from Canvas less than 5 minutes before the scheduled start time saying the class would be moved online or cancelled due to “other commitments.” Several of us had already arrived on campus by then, having commuted 40–60 minutes.

For example, on 2026-04-02 (Week 4), I arrived at the Swanston Academic Building at 10:20am. At 10:27am, a Canvas announcement was posted saying the class would be asynchronous that day and we should “work independently on the lab sheet.” No Zoom link or alternative support was provided. A few of us waited in the lab room (08.09.25) until about 11:00am, but the teacher never appeared. This has impacted my ability to properly follow the material, especially as I rely on in-lab explanations to understand Python concepts.

2. Inconsistent and unclear assessment instructions
There have been multiple instances where the written assignment instructions on Canvas did not match what was said verbally in class, and where the marking rubric appears to have been applied differently than what was communicated.

For Assignment 1 (due 2026-04-18), the brief on Canvas initially stated that we should implement 3 functions according to the provided specifications. In Week 5’s lab (2026-03-29), Mr. Carter verbally told us we could either implement the full menu-driven program or just the 3 functions, and that both options would receive full marks if the functions worked correctly. However, when grades were released, many students (including me) lost marks because we “did not implement a user menu,” even though that was described as optional in class.

When I emailed Mr. Carter on 2026-04-22 to query this, his reply (copied below) was brief and felt dismissive:

“Hi Emily,
The expectations were clearly outlined in the assessment brief. We need to encourage students to go beyond the minimum. Marks stand.
Regards,
John”

I went back and checked the original published brief and there was no mention that the menu was compulsory. Several classmates remember the same verbal explanation. This inconsistency has made it difficult to trust what is being said in class versus what will be graded.

3. Communication style and professionalism
I understand that lecturers are busy and under pressure, but on several occasions Mr. Carter’s tone, both in class and over email, has felt dismissive and, at times, slightly mocking when students struggle.

Specific examples:
- In Week 6 (2026-04-12), when a student asked for a third time for clarification on list comprehensions, Mr. Carter said in front of the class, “If you’re still not getting this, you really need to reconsider if programming is for you.” The student looked visibly embarrassed and stopped asking questions for the rest of the lab.
- In Week 7 (2026-04-19), I stayed back after class to explain that I’m balancing part-time work (about 20 hours a week at a café) and asked if he had any recommendations for prioritising which lab exercises were most important when I’m short on time. He responded, “You just have to want it enough. Other students manage full-time work and still get HDs.” This made me feel as though my situation was being dismissed rather than supported.
- Several of his replies on the Canvas discussion board are quite blunt. For instance, when another student asked if they could submit a slightly late lab due to a medical appointment, he replied publicly, “Deadlines are there for a reason. Plan ahead.” This was before the student had a chance to upload a medical certificate or message privately.

I don’t believe any of these comments were intended as outright bullying, but the pattern has created an environment where many of us feel reluctant to ask for help. A few classmates have mentioned in our group chat that they now “just Google things instead of asking” because they don’t want to be made to feel stupid.

4. Unequal treatment in extensions and marking
There appear to be inconsistencies in how extensions and late penalties are being applied. For Assignment 2, I requested a 48-hour extension on 2026-05-03 due to a flare-up of a chronic migraine condition (I attached a medical certificate from my GP, Dr. Patel, covering 2026-05-02 to 2026-05-05). My request was denied with the explanation that “extensions are only for serious or unexpected circumstances,” despite my condition being documented and unpredictable.

However, two classmates mentioned (in our private group chat) that they were granted informal extensions of 24–48 hours after explaining to Mr. Carter that they were “just overwhelmed” and “had a busy week.” I understand this is hearsay, but one of them showed me a screenshot of his email granting them a 24-hour extension without any documentation requested. I felt this was unfair, especially since I followed the formal process and had medical evidence.

Additionally, in my Assignment 2 feedback, I lost marks for “not following PEP8 naming conventions,” yet in class Mr. Carter had repeatedly said we should “aim for PEP8, but we won’t be super strict on it in first year.” My variable names were descriptive (e.g., totalScore, userInputString) and functional. I’ve observed code examples from other students in class that use similar naming and they did not receive the same comment in their feedback.

Impact on my learning and wellbeing
The combination of last-minute cancellations, unclear expectations, and the communication style has significantly increased my stress this semester. I am a first-year international student (from Vietnam), and this is my first semester at RMIT. Programming Fundamentals is a core course for my Bachelor of Information Technology, so it is particularly worrying that I feel discouraged and, at times, anxious about attending this class.

On days when classes have been cancelled last minute, I have wasted time and money commuting into the city unnecessarily. I also work part-time to support myself, so reorganising my work shifts around classes that then do not run has been difficult. I have started to doubt my ability in programming, even though in high school I did reasonably well in similar subjects.

Steps I have already taken
- I have spoken informally with two classmates (Sarah and Anish) who share similar concerns about the class environment and the inconsistent instructions.
- I sent two polite emails to Mr. Carter (on 2026-04-22 and 2026-05-03) raising my concerns about the assessment instructions and my extension request. His responses were brief and did not address the fairness or clarity issues I raised.
- I approached the course coordinator, Dr. Melissa Chan, briefly after a lecture in Week 9 (2026-05-10) to mention some of these concerns. She suggested that if I felt strongly about it, I could submit a formal complaint so that there would be a proper record.

What I am requesting
1. A review of the communication and cancellation practices for this lab group, including clearer minimum notice times for cancellations or changes, and ensuring that alternative support (e.g., recorded content, online consultation) is provided when classes are cancelled.
2. A review of the assessment instructions and marking for COSC2627 Assignment 1 and 2, specifically for Group L02, to ensure that the expectations communicated verbally and in writing are aligned, and that marks are being applied consistently.
3. Consideration of staff development or guidance for Mr. Carter around communication style with first-year students, especially regarding how feedback and advice are phrased so that students are not discouraged from asking for help.
4. If possible, an opportunity for me (and others affected) to have our assignments re-reviewed by another marker or the course coordinator, particularly where there is evidence that instructions were ambiguous or changed verbally.

I want to emphasise that I do not think Mr. Carter is intentionally trying to disadvantage students, but the overall pattern of behaviour and inconsistencies has made it harder to succeed in this course than I believe is reasonable. I am submitting this complaint in the hope that these issues can be addressed constructively so that future students do not have the same experience, and so that my own learning this semester can be fairly assessed.
